summary: Gruber revisits Clayton Christensen's failed 2007 prediction that the iPhone would not succeed, arguing that Christensen's error was in categorizing the iPhone as a sustaining innovation in the phone market rather than a disruptive one in the computer market. A New Yorker profile of Christensen confirmed this: the iPhone was disruptive to laptops, not phones. Gruber argues the iPhone was never truly a phone but a pocket-sized computer that happened to include phone functionality. He traces Apple's preparation through a decade of building portable computers and pocketable devices. The result was not disruption of the handset industry but its destruction, as cell phones became apps rather than devices. The piece concludes that even the name 'iPhone' misdirected competitors and analysts into underestimating its true nature.
key_points: Christensen's disruption theory failed on the iPhone because he categorized it as a better phone rather than recognizing it as disruptive to the computer industry
Apple's decade of building portable computers and iPods were training exercises for the iPhone, not its phone expertise
The iPhone destroyed the handset industry not by being a better phone but by making computers permanently pocketable
The 'phone' in 'iPhone' was effective marketing but misdirected analysts and competitors into underestimating its revolutionary nature
Pre-iPhone phone leaders like Palm and RIM are dead or dying because they were phone makers, not computer makers
money_quotes: The iPhone is not and never was a phone. It is a pocket-sized computer that obviates the phone.
The iPhone is to cell phones what the Mac was to typewriters.
The disruption was not that we now finally had a nice phone; it was that, for better or for worse, we would now never again be without a computer or the Internet.
What's happened over the last five years shows not that Apple disrupted the phone handset industry, but rather that Apple destroyed the handset industry — by disrupting the computer industry.
Today, cell phones are apps, not devices.
It was the Mac side of Apple, not the iPod side, that set the engineering foundation for the iPhone.
By framing it merely as a cool new phone rather than a revolutionary new computing platform, Apple positioned the iPhone as something people in the mass market already thought they wanted.
key_insight: The iPhone's genius was not in disrupting the phone industry but in destroying it entirely by putting a real computer in everyone's pocket.
